result.markdown:
--- title: "Neurosymbolic solver for deductive trading" jupyter: brainvomit --- ## Thesis A prediction market sells **contracts** that pay \$1 if some speculatory thing happens and \$0 if it doesn't, so the price is the market's economic estimate of the probability. The known ways to make risk-free money here -- prices that don't add up, or the same bet going cheaper on another site -- get washed instantaneously once the megafunds arrive. As a bottomfeeder, I relinquished hope for a different kind of arbitrage altogether. Every contract has stated **terms**, the legal authority that decides who gets paid. The idea: have a language model read those terms and translate them into formal logic, then hand the logic (DSL) to an automated theorem-prover (SMT solver) that can prove when two+ contracts' prices contradict each other. A proven contradiction is a risk-free trade. The arbitrage, if found, lives within the fine print no one bothers to read. Two things had to arrive together: **markets that trade yes/no claims** (SAT solvers are irreducibly boolean solvers), and **cheap reading of dense legal text** (LLMs). **Induction** learns patterns from data ("it went up the last ten times") — what essentially all quantitative trading does. **Deduction** proves something *must* follow from rules ("if one party controls the House, the Speaker is necessarily from that party"). Prediction markets are the first asset class where deductive incongruence is exploitable, because they trade boolean claims that are logically coupled. There's also a reason we *must* stay with deduction: a market event happens once, and for a one-time event a single "true probability" is ill-defined. What *is* well-defined is **coherence** — whether a set of prices contradict each other right now. ## Example scenario A more intuitive and topical example: consider the following World Cup final round schedule: | Team | Points | Final match | | --- | --- | --- | | Brazil | 6 | vs Cameroon | | Serbia | 3 | vs Switzerland | | Switzerland | 3 | vs Serbia | | Cameroon | 0 | vs Brazil | The encoded boolean model is simply: - **each remaining match** is one unknown — home win / draw / away win — paying `(3, 0)` / `(1, 1)` / `(0, 3)`; - **final points** = points now + what those unknowns add; - **clinched** ⟺ `UNSAT(2+ rivals can finish at or above it)` — no result leaves it outside the top two; - **eliminated** ⟺ `UNSAT(≤1 rival finishes strictly above it)` — every result leaves it behind two or more. - **fewest points to clinch** — the smallest final total `P` for which `UNSAT(finish on ≥ P and 2+ rivals still at or above)` holds: the lowest total that guarantees a top-two seat. If no reachable `P` works, the team can't clinch at all. Running the solver on this scenario: ::: {#6c260544 .cell execution_count=1} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Brazil clinched Serbia still alive Switzerland still alive Cameroon eliminated Cameroon eliminated even if it beats Brazil: True ``` ::: ::: What isn't obvious from the score table: - **Brazil has clinched before its own match.** Serbia and Switzerland each sit on 3 and could climb to 6, but they play *each other*, so at most one gets there and the other is left behind. No set of results puts two teams above Brazil, so it is through even if it loses to Cameroon. - **Cameroon is eliminated even if it beats Brazil.** A win lifts it only to 4, and the Serbia–Switzerland game always leaves two teams ahead — so its fate is sealed by a match it isn't even in. Both are facts the table doesn't show: the scheduling produces relationships that are forced yet non-obvious, and the solver just retrieves them. The same model can be used to answer another question: *what is the fewest points that guarantee qualification?* Rewind to just **after the first round** of group games: Brazil beat Cameroon, Serbia and Switzerland drew, and two rounds remain outstanding: | Team | Points | Played | Remaining matches | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Brazil | 3 | beat Cameroon | vs Serbia, vs Switzerland | | Serbia | 1 | drew Switzerland | vs Brazil, vs Cameroon | | Switzerland | 1 | drew Serbia | vs Brazil, vs Cameroon | | Cameroon | 0 | lost to Brazil | vs Serbia, vs Switzerland | ::: {#d9233a4f .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Brazil clinches with 6 points (can finish on [3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9]) Serbia clinches with 7 points (can finish on [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7]) Switzerland clinches with 7 points (can finish on [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7]) Cameroon clinches with 6 points (can finish on [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6]) ``` ::: ::: The surprise is Brazil's **6** — guaranteed through on six points even though Serbia *and* Switzerland can each still finish on **7, above it**. The escape is hidden in the schedule: the only way either chaser reaches 7 is to win out, and both of their run-ins include the match *against Brazil* — so Brazil reaching 6 means it has already beaten one of them, and the two can't both finish above it. Serbia and Switzerland, by contrast, must win out to their maximum of 7 to be safe; drop a single point and a tie for the second seat survives. Even Cameroon, beaten in its opener, isn't eliminated — it can still clinch, but only by taking all six from its two games. From here, swap "tournament rules" for "contract rulebooks" and "standings" for "live prices." ## Strategy The expensive step -- carefully reading legal text, turning it into logical statements, checking them with the solver -- is spent only on promising candidates, identified first by cheap heuristics: 1. liquity check: is it actually being traded? 2. encode terms into logical statements, then check the solver for USNATs 3. degrees-of-freedom-removed: score how much the added terms reduce the set of possible outcomes 4. grow the set toward whatever imposes the strongest constraints, then check the solver again Whether a search is even worth starting is decided up front by a break-even rule: the fraction of groups that must contain a tradeable find to justify the cost. $$p_1 = \frac{C_\text{cluster}}{V_\text{find}\times R}$$ where $C_\text{cluster}$ is the cost to examine one group, $V_\text{find}$ the profit per find (capped by how much the market can absorb), and $R$ how many times one discovery pays off (repeatability.) ## The golden case A few attributes have been identified to be necessary (but not sufficient) for a mispricing to be *tradeable*: 1. The relationship must be logically forced by the legal terms 2. **True no matter how the world turns out**; unintuitively, outcomes are not determined by the premise but by the fine print of the rulebooks. This is where bet resolution becomes debatable. 3. **hidden across contracts**: visible only by reading two or more together; ie the contradiction is not within itself (too obvious) but between two or more contracts 4. **contradicted by the actual tradeable prices** — with real volume on every leg at once 5. Conditions about history, outside parallels, and the market consensus all collapse into one question: **does anyone already watch this relationship here?** If yes, it's priced. So the target is one *nobody* watches — which makes any such opportunity temporary. One rule decides the "true no matter what" condition: **a forced link survives when both contracts resolve on a yes/no event and breaks the moment either resolves on a continuous number. ** A continuous strike (a vote share, an inflation reading) makes the exact number the operative fact, so for some values the implication between two contracts simply fails. A yes/no fact (a law passed, a person appointed) is shared verbatim, so the implication holds automatically. ## From rulebook to proof The fuzzy half (reading) and the provable half (checking) are kept strictly apart. For example, a real Fed contract reads: > "If the upper bound of the target federal funds rate … is greater than 5.25% > following the Fed's Mar 2026 meeting, then the market resolves Yes." The model's only job is to turn that sentence into a strutctured representation: ```json { "ticker": "KXFED-26MAR-T5.25", "var": "level", "op": ">", "value": 5.25, "at": "26MAR", "grounding": "adjudicator" } ``` A deterministic step (DSL) then lowers that into the solver's language (SMT): ```scheme (declare-const level@26MAR Real) (assert (> level@26MAR 5.25)) ; pays Yes ⇔ this holds ``` **A relationship is proven exactly when its negation can't be satisfied.** The whole engine rests on one check — a claim $p$ is *forced* by a set of contracts $S$ iff $$\textsf{UNSAT}\big(\;\text{axioms}\;\wedge\;\textstyle\bigwedge S\;\wedge\;\neg p\;\big)$$ — no world consistent with the rulebooks makes the premises true and the claim false. **Real verdicts**, each a single solver call on a known-answer pair: | Premise(s) → claim | Result | Meaning | | --- | --- | --- | | *Republicans win the House by ≥10 seats* → *Republicans win the House* | **UNSAT** | proven; prices must obey P(≥10) ≤ P(win) | | *Mamdani wins NYC mayor* ∧ *Cuomo wins NYC mayor* | **UNSAT** | can't both hold (one winner) → P(A) + P(B) ≤ 1 | | *Trump renames the Gulf* → *a Trump appointee is held in contempt* | **SAT** | **not** proven — solver returns a world where the first holds, the second fails; topical overlap only | **UNSAT** is the prize: the statement is verifiably **true**, so any prices that break it are a locked mispricing. **SAT** is the trap-detector — it returns an explicit counterexample, exposing a "link" that was only topical (the independence trap that kills most naive matches). **UNKNOWN** is the third outcome, and it is informative. When a payout turns on *where a continuous number lands* — a threshold like `level > 5.25` that must be reconciled with another contract's `change == 0.25` — the implication can't be decided without extra axioms tying levels to changes, and the solver returns UNKNOWN. A yes/no event never does this. That gap is the [continuous-number trap](#the-golden-case) in its most literal form: the logic that holds for discrete events dissolves the moment a free real number sits between the contracts. ## Findings ### 1. Single contracts — dead In a 20-contract sample, about half had a gap between headline and fine print; **none were tradeable** (each gap was harmless or already priced). Reading one contract is easy, so the edge is gone. ### 2. Economics & finance — effectively dead Nearly every economics/finance contract also trades elsewhere (rate futures, yields, official series), where professionals keep it priced — the **outside-copy killer**. Sorted by whether an outside copy exists, the catalog splits ~3,674 dead (Sports 2,221, Economics 572, Financials 568, Crypto 253, Commodities 60) against ~7,287 native to Kalshi. The fertile ground is the native side — **politics and legal events especially**, where one official ruling gets described across very different contracts and the link hides in the fine print. ### 3. Contracts about the exact same number — rare A 165-contract scan found essentially zero genuine matches on the *literal same measurement* across families; most apparent matches just cite the same news source. Expected — the real target is a definitional link between *different* measurements, which by construction won't show up in a search for identical ones. ### 4. Sports — good structure, killed by other exchanges Sports has the best raw structure (many related yes/no bets on one game) and dodges the continuous-number trap — but every bet also exists at a sportsbook, which keeps the set priced. *Sports is economics with a yes/no coat of paint.* No category has "native + hidden + recurring" all at once, so the target is a rare specific **pair** of contracts, not a whole category — which is why a targeted hand-search beats betting on a category. ### 5. The life and death of a real arbitrage The strongest find pairs two contracts that look unrelated by their titles: a **parlay on who controls each chamber** (the four House×Senate combinations) and a **direct bet on which party controls the House**. Read together, the combos *partition* every outcome, and "Democrats control the House" is exactly two of them — so the direct contract must equal the DD-or-DR bundle. The solver proves that identity, then the real Kalshi prices go in: ::: {#3ab5d985 .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` CONTROLH-D == DD ∨ DR : forced (UNSAT of its negation) bundle DD+DR = 0.76 vs direct bid 0.78 -> 2¢ incoherence locked basket costs 0.98 -> +2¢ gross, −4.5¢ fees, net -2.5¢ ``` ::: ::: So the identity is **proven**; the legs are deeply liquid (real Kalshi open interest: \$4.1M / \$990k / \$930k, ~1¢ spreads); two different crowds trade the two framings — and there really was a 2¢ gap. It is the first concrete proof the target class is non-empty. It just doesn't pay: the per-leg fee (~4.5¢ over three legs) is larger than the gap. That's the general trap — a *liquid* mispricing is forced tiny, while a *large* one needs a leg so mispriced it's either untradeable (thin) or merely slow (a speed edge, not a logic edge). The profitable middle is squeezed shut. **Was it real, and how long did it last?** Pulling the three legs' full daily history from Kalshi — since the combo legs began trading in December 2025 — and plotting the gap (direct bid − bundle ask) over its whole life: ::: {#5be0f6b1 .cell execution_count=4} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: For almost its entire life the gap sat **at or below zero** — the bundle was as cheap as or cheaper than the direct contract, exactly as coherence demands — drifting in a −1¢ to −4¢ band. It poked into arbitrage territory only in brief, shallow bursts: **eight short windows in six months, every one ≤ 2¢ and none lasting more than five days.** Most were a single 1¢ day; the two genuine 2¢ events were a lone spike on **Apr 1** (the direct contract jumped to 86¢ a day ahead of the bundle, gone the next day when the DD leg caught up) and the **Jun 19–23** run analysed above — which closed on Jun 24 when the D-House/R-Senate leg repriced 36¢ → 38¢ and the bundle rose to meet the 78¢ bid. That is the thesis in one picture: a *liquid*, closely-watched relationship never lets incoherence grow past a couple of cents or survive a week. The June window wasn't special — just the longest of several sub-fee blips. Each was a real, provable mispricing; none was large enough to clear the ~4.5¢ round-trip fee. (Verified live: the [Balance-of-Power combo legs](https://kalshi.com/markets/kxbalancepowercombo) and the "Democrats win the House" market are still open on Kalshi, now coherent.) **Conclusion:** can't quit my day job.
